title: Kids compete in junior games
summary: For some kids, a little encouragement goes a long way. Long way, as in halfway across a gym when you're hopping in a push-up position during the Junior Native Youth Olympics. Brandon Wright of Ursa Major Elementary wowed the crowd with a 72-foot, 3-inch performance to win the Grade 1-2 Boys Seal Hop at the annual event Jan. 18 at Fairview Recreation Center. He really wowed his own father, said Ben Snowball, coordinator of the games. "His dad was on the sidelines encouraging him to go on like a proud father. (Brandon) was so happy from the thought of his dad, that that gave him the determination to go on." The games are based on tradition, and skills necessary to fare well in activities such as hunting or boating. Seal Hop is a game of endurance -- to see how far a person can go on pure det…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | GTL plant in Nikiski on track BP: Pilot unit may start testing gas conversion technology in April. The Associated Press (Published: March 2, 2002) Anchorage -- A pilot plant in Nikiski for testing BP's technology to convert natural gas to liquid fuels is on track to start by April, a project manager said. The $86 million plant will be used to test new technology BP has developed, including a compact reformer that's about a fortieth the size of a conventional unit. About $40 million of the total was spent in Alaska. Testing at Nikiski could go on as long as five years, according to Steve Fortune, the project's engineering manager. GTL engineering manager Steve Fortune said the technology designed around the unit is more efficient and more environmentally sound. About 100 to 120 worker…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: Howard Paul Thomas of Anchorage received the 2001 Harold R. Peyton Award from the American Society of Civil Engineers. He was recognized for his work in cold regions engineering at the annual Civil Engineering Conference and Exposition in Houston last fall. Anchorage School District psychologist Deborah Ward is one of three finalists for the national school psychologist of the year award presented by the National Association of School Psychologists. Ward, who has been a school psychologist in Alaska since 1983, works at Bowman Elementary School and Steller Secondary School. Alma Upicksoun was recently appointed to the Alaska Native Heritage Center's board of directors for a three-year term. She joins the 15-member board as one of five directors representing Alaska Native regional corporati…

title: New NMFS chief takes on agency beset with lawsuits
summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service is an agency people love to hate. You can tell by all the lawsuits pending against it. The agency, based in Silver Spring, Md., regulates fish harvests off the U.S. coast. It also works to preserve marine mammal populations and marine habitat. Its Juneau-based office regulates the largest commercial seafood harvest in the nation, with Alaska fishermen landing about half the 9.1 billion pounds of U.S. commercial landings in 2000. Vast as they are, the ocean riches are hardly inexhaustible. Nobody knows that more than William Hogarth, a career fish scientist with a North Carolina drawl whom the Bush administration appointed in September to head the Fisheries Service. He stepped into a job whose staff of biologists and other researchers is increasingly di…

title: Slow drivers may face higher fines
summary: Juneau -- Driving too slowly could cost more than driving too fast under a bill that passed in the state Senate on Monday. The Senate voted 17-1 in favor of a bill to increase the fine for drivers who let five or more cars line up behind them from $30 to $200. The bill's sponsor, Sen. Dave Donley, R-Anchorage, said the law would apply only if the road in front of the driver is unobstructed, if he is traveling under the speed limit and if it's safe for him to pull over to let others pass. Donley said it's unfair and dangerous for a few drivers to "own" the road and set their own speed limit. "Unlike the Lower 48, Alaska doesn't have an extensive four-lane highway system," Donley said. The problem is particularly bad on the Seward Highway leading from Anchorage to the Kenai Peninsula, where …

title: Mallards, sparrows top 23rd Mat-Su bird count
summary: Rosemary Vigdahl sees lots of birds from her window overlooking Bodenburg Creek. Feeders around her home at Mile 13.25 Old Glenn Highway draw a variety of species, and bald eagles perch in the spruce trees along the creek. The open water once attracted, among other rare winter visitors, a great blue heron. During the Mat-Su Valley Christmas Bird Count on Dec. 30, she was tallying mallards on the creek when a great horned owl swooped in for lunch. "He sat on the bank right below my window, munching the duck. The sun was up. It wasn't nighttime. He had no qualms about it." About halfway through his entree, the owl must have gotten nervous dining 10 feet from prying eyes, Vigdahl said. He ferried his prey to the other side of the creek to finish it. Vigdahl was one of 14 feeder watchers who p…

title: Courtroom stands out in otherwise Spartan building
summary: Don't tell anyone, but the built-in courtroom at the new Anchorage Jail is quite attractive. Really small but attractive -- all white maple with dark cherry wood accents. That sets it apart from the rest of the $58 million building, which is designed to hold 400 prisoners and scheduled to open for business April 2. The jail, on Fourth Avenue next to Cook Inlet Pre-Trial Facility, is owned by the city but will be leased to and run by the state. Mindful of cost, and the grumbling that accompanies spending money to house criminals, designers of the jail opted for techno-Spartan: bare cement floors and bulletproof glass, whitewashed concrete block walls and big steel doors, electronic central controls and cameras everywhere. It's not ugly. There's a certain beauty in clean lines and, at the mo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Tree-Cutting Rules Deck your halls with a free tree; go cut your own (Published: December 9, 2001) Cut only on designated U.S. Forest Service land south of Turnagain Pass and on state land north of Palmer. All cutting is banned on Kenai Peninsula and Mat-su borough land. Obtain maps for these areas by contacting the U.S. Forest Service, the state Department of Natural Resources or by going to the Alaska Public Lands Center at the corner of Fourth Avenue and F Street, which has both federal and state land information. Cutting a tree on city property or in Chugach State Park is illegal. Fines range as high as $200. Cutting must be at least 450 feet from any roads, trails, trail heads, campgrounds or body of water. That's 11/2 football fields. Stumps must be cut low to the grown and all…

title: House OKs adding verse to song
summary: Juneau -- The House agreed unanimously Wednesday to add a verse honoring the contributions of Alaska Natives to the official state song. The bill's sponsor, Rep. Carl Morgan, R-Aniak, said adopting the second verse will help heal wounds and unite the state. "Alaska's Flag" was written by Marie Drake and officially adopted as the state song in 1956. Carol Beery Davis wrote the second verse in the 1980s, but an attempt in 1987 to officially add it to the state song failed. Morgan said the change is timely because this year marks the 75th anniversary of the Alaska flag. The flag was the creation of Benny Benson, an Alaska Native youth who won a contest for its design in 1927. The Governor's Commission on Tolerance last fall recommended adding the verse to as a way to heal racial divisions. Th…

title: Subsistence hearing to be rescheduled
summary: The national crisis has put on hold the Alaska Federation of Natives' effort to take its subsistence fight to Congress. Speaking at the start of the AFN convention in Anchorage Thursday, AFN president Julie Kitka said a congressional hearing on subsistence scheduled for Nov. 1 has been scratched and will be rescheduled. She said the office of the Senate Indian Affairs Committee, which planned to hold the hearing, is still closed down because of anthrax testing. The committee had agreed to hold a hearing on possible new protections for rural or Native subsistence users under federal law. VILLAGE SCOUT BATTALIONS The war against terrorism could have another effect on Alaska village life, the AFN convention was told. It's time to strengthen the scout battalions of the National Guard, Adjutant…

title: Armed robberies reported at Subway, Midtown Lodge
summary: ANCHORAGE Armed robberies reported at Subway, Midtown Lodge Police were investigating two armed robberies Thursday night in Midtown. In the first incident, the robber failed to get any money from a Subway after the employees ran out the back door when they saw his handgun. Police were called at 8:47 p.m. to the Subway adjacent the Williams & Kay furniture store at Seward Highway and Northern Lights Boulevard. Officers found three employees hiding in one of their vehicles in the rear parking lot. The robber had fled. Sgt. Rick Shell said one employee was up front when the robber, wearing a black jacket and black face mask, entered and brandished a silver handgun. She dashed for the back room, where two others were working, and all three went out the rear door to take cover in the car. "It d…

title: Woman hurt in sledding accident
summary: A 25-year-old woman being towed in a plastic sled down Quartz Creek Road in Cooper Landing suffered major head injuries Saturday after she skidded into the oncoming lane and collided with a pickup, according to the Alaska State Troopers. Deanna Hoy of Cooper Landing was listed in fair condition Sunday night at Providence Alaska Medical Center. Hoy was riding in a sled being towed northbound behind a 1993 Jeep driven by 28-year-old Coleen Clarke through a series of curves near Mile 1 about 4:30 p.m. when Clarke and passenger Erick Fish, 25, encountered southbound traffic, troopers reported. Clarke slowed the Jeep, but Hoy and the sled crossed into the path of a Chevrolet pickup driven by Steven Skolnick, 43, of Cooper Landing. Cooper Landing emergency medical crews responded to the scene, a…

title: Swedish mushers overcome logistical nightmares to make Alaska home
summary: For two people always in a hurry to cross the finish line, Egil Ellis and Helen Lundberg sure are procrastinators. They have won some of the world's biggest sprint sled dog races. They have beaten top rivals and the clock. But moving from Sweden to Alaska before snowfall was a different race. Their hearts were in the right place, their plans precise. Get to Willow by August. Take time to move in before starting training. But their execution was off. This wasn't a three-day sprint race, like the Fur Rendezvous Open World Championship, in which Ellis today begins defense of his title. This was a marathon involving thousands of miles, 100 dogs, two lives' worth of clutter and one delay after another. Ellis and Lundberg had crossed paths many times along the European racing circuit in the mid-…

title: Natives gather for convention
summary: The largest gathering of Alaska Natives in the state gets under way Monday with a focus on subsistence and racism and the air of a family reunion. About 3,000 Eskimos, Indians and Aleuts are expected at the annual convention of the Alaska Federation of Natives, the 36th since 1966. This is no stodgy dentists' convention: Elders get seats of honor in the front of the room, kids are welcome, and a Native arts and crafts sale bustles downstairs. The week's events begin when the Elders and Youth Conference convenes on Monday, and continues until the AFN delegates wrap up action on a series of resolutions Saturday. But business will also get conducted in the rear of the packed auditorium at the Egan Center and out in the halls. "We keep trying to shoo them out but there's still that visiting th…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: SCHOOL LUNCH MENU MONDAY Anchorage: Elementary school -- pepperoni pizza or Belgian waffle with sausage links or corn dog, Orange Smiles, celery sticks, milk. Middle school -- Domino's pizza with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, bread, milk. Mat-Su: Beef and bean burrito, chilled canned fruit, carrot sticks with ranch dip, milk (white or strawberry). TODAY COMMUNITY Anchorage Widowed Persons support group for newly widowed persons, 1:30 p.m., Anchorage Senior Center, 1300 E. 19th Ave. Men and women widowed for longer periods of time are also welcome to attend. (566-0580) Dudes and Dames Club Midwinter Festival, with workshops and dances, continues 11 a.m.-7 p.m., Square and Round Dance Center, 432 Harvard Ave., Government Hill -- $6 each class, $6-$8 dances, $40 all. (278-9…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Oil spills at three pipeline stations ALYESKA: Procedural bust' arises during annual maintenance shutdown. By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 26, 2001) Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. grappled with three oil spills Saturday after the company attempted to restart the trans-Alaska pipeline. As much as 1,200 gallons of oil may have spilled, according to regulators who oversee the pipe. The oil didn't reach land near the pipeline, but regulators say the missteps that led to the spills are among the most serious problems along the pipeline in recent years. "This is the biggest procedural bust at Alyeska in a long time," said Jerry Brossia, with the joint state-federal Joint Pipeline Office, which oversees pipeline operations. "They just got sloppy." The spills stem from…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | ANWR debate spotlights Hussein OIL Murkowski offers amendment to stop imports from Iraq. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 24, 2002) Washington -- The specter of Saddam Hussein is never far away when Sen. Frank Murkowski and his allies argue for drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge. Sen. Larry Craig invoked the Iraqi dictator's name at a recent ANWR press conference on the Capitol lawn. The Idaho Republican asked who had gassed up their cars that morning. "If you did, then you contributed to the terrorist activity of Saddam Hussein. Thank you," he said darkly. At another ANWR event in the same location, Republicans brought a giant poster of Hussein that included a ticking electronic counter. "Since the House passed the energy bill, America has purchased …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Flying priest missing JIM KELLEY: Plane may have gone down on mountain 25 miles from Dillingham. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 24, 2002) The Catholic parish priest for Western Alaska is missing, and searchers say his single-engine plane might have gone down on the way to Togiak. The Rev. Jim Kelley, who was alone, left Dillingham on Saturday morning, preparing for a series of Palm Sunday Masses today around Bristol Bay. By Saturday night, the cleric and his Piper Cherokee PA28-140 still hadn't arrived. Heavy fog and snow were hampering an intensive search that included about 15 local volunteer search parties on snowmachines and an Army National Guard Black Hawk helicopter from Bethel. A C-130 carrying Air National Guard pararescuers was en route from Kulis A…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Diomede mail run is often a white-knuckle ride TRICKY: Helicopter braves challenging weather to make deliveries to island village. By James Macpherson Alaska Journal Of Commerce (Published: March 24, 2002) Years after man walked on the moon, walrus-skinned boats still delivered mail to Diomede. Occasionally, a cargo plane would fly over the tiny island village 28 miles off the Seward Peninsula. Sacks of mail would be thrown out, tumbling hundreds of feet to the tundra. "We couldn't order fine china," said Francis Ozenna, city manager of Diomede. The village didn't get regular mail service until 1982. Except for one year when it was underbid, Evergreen Helicopters of Alaska has provided the weekly mail runs to Diomede, 135 miles northwest of Nome. The postal contract is one of the old…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | The Puppeteer Chuck Hamel pulls all strings to keep oil giant BP honest By Tony Hopfinger Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 24, 2002) Chuck Hamel carries a worn leather briefcase, along with a big chip on his shoulder, when he swings through Anchorage to check on his war against oil behemoth BP. The Virginia man meets with BP's criminal probation officer. He visits a lawyer whom he might ask to help in his fight. And he stops by the newspaper, trolling stories of a company, he claims, is not committed to the environment and worker safety at the huge Prudhoe Bay oil field. Hamel beams devilishly, patting his briefcase as though it contains a secret weapon. He is pulling strings like a master puppeteer, just as he's done for more than 17 years. Hamel is the Ralph Nader of Alaska's…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Vanishing tracks Snowshoe artisans among last to follow traditional Athabaskan technique By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 24, 2002) Dennis holds caribou hide strips that he used in his early years of making snowshoes. He now uses nylon. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) At his Nikolai home, Nick Dennis sits with the tools he uses to make snowshoes. He may be the last Native known to build snowshoes using traditional methods and design. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) Nick Dennis travels by snowmachine to the outskirts of the tiny Native village of Nikolai looking for rare stands of birch. Two hundred miles north in Huslia, George Yaska scans ridgetops for birch stands during the fall moose hunt. The two Native snowshoe artisans do…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaskans honored for their contributions to state BANQUET Committee recognizes Dan Cuddy as Alaskan of The Year. Daily News Staff Staff (Published: March 24, 2002) Several Alaskans were recognized at the Alaskan of the Year banquet Saturday at the Sheraton Anchorage Hotel. Each year since 1967, the Alaskan of the Year Committee has honored people who have contributed to the state. A list of this year's honorees follows. 2002 ALASKAN OF THE YEAR: Dan Cuddy, bank chairman and president of First National Bank Alaska. This award is for outstanding contributions that have significantly affected the character and development of this state. Cuddy, 81, was born in Valdez. He received a bachelor of science degree from Stanford University and studied law at the University of Washington in Seat…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Wade prosecution finds a break ESTIMONY: FBI agent heard important parts of murder suspect's taped conversation. By Sheila Toomey Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 23, 2002) Fortune finally cut the prosecution a break in its battle to use taped incriminating statements made by Joshua Wade at his upcoming trial for murder. An FBI agent testified Friday during an extended pre-trial evidence hearing that she heard significant parts of the taped conversation, during which Wade said he killed Della Brown. Brown's body was discovered on Sept. 2, 2000, in a shack on Dorbrandt Street in Spenard. Wade, 22, was arrested a month later, after two informants engaged him in a conversation about the murder while riding in a truck and wearing police bugs. Although the conversation was captured …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Three Dudes ice carving contest FIRST PLACE: Anchorage team wins U.S. National Snow Sculpting Competition. By Rose Cox Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 23, 2002) Strategy and persistence paid off for the Three Dudes from Anchorage during the recent U.S. National Snow Sculpting Competition in Wisconsin. First, they scaled back their design to better fit the three-day time limit. They carved at night to combat unseasonably warm weather in Lake Geneva, where the competition has been held in recent years. A misplaced chisel at the end of the first day cost carver Gary Keeton 14 stitches in his right hand, but the team still managed to finish in time. The Dudes -- Keeton, Les Babcock and Gary Lyon -- took Grand Champion at the competition for their sculpture, "A Strong Spirit," feat…

title: Travel & Business info
summary: In Alaska Outdoors News Field Notes (Published: January 13, 2002) DEADLINES Last day for Carrs Christmas tree drop-off Today is the last day to get your Christmas tree into the recycling heap. Trees may be dropped off at any Carrs store. Remove all lights and ornaments first. There are clearly marked areas in each parking lot for you to drop off your tree. The recycled trees are processed into wood chips and distributed to local nonprofit and government agencies for use in compost and on trails, paths and gardens. For information, contact Alpar at 274-3266. FISHING Sportfish stocking comments due Jan. 15 Comments on sportfish stocking at Anchorage's two state-owned hatcheries which raise king and silver salmon, rainbow trout, grayling and Arctic char are due Jan. 15. The Division of Sport …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Black museum lacks staff, closes FAIRBANKS: Volunteers couldn't meet Alaskaland rule. The Associated Press (Published: March 21, 2002) Fairbanks -- The African-American Community Museum at Alaskaland won't be open this summer because the museum has too few volunteers. The museum's operators -- members of the Fairbanks Black Chamber of Commerce -- could not keep the doors to the museum open the prescribed number of hours. The museum's rental contract with Alaskaland, therefore, won't be renewed. The rule at Alaskaland is that shops must be open all hours between 11 a.m. and 9 p.m. "Consistency is important with Alaskaland," said North Star Borough Mayor Rhonda Boyles. "If you've got tourists going there, what does it say if half the shops are closed at 7 at night?" The chamber is look…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Four teachers win top award SCHOOLS: Math, science instructors recognized with nation's highest honor. The Associated Press (Published: March 21, 2002) Four Alaskans are among the teachers who received the nation's highest honor for mathematics and science instruction. The elementary and middle school teachers were picked for Presidential Awards for Excellence in Mathematics and Science Teaching handed out by the National Science Foundation. Alaska's recipients are Ruth Mount, a math teacher from Mirror Lake Middle School in Chugiak; Lisa Stewart, a math teacher from Swanson Elementary School in Palmer; Sheryl Sotelo, a science teacher from Cooper Landing Elementary School; and Ronald Reihl, a science teacher at Tanana Middle School in Fairbanks. The four will receive a $7,500 grant …

title: Congress to hear subsistence issue
summary: Alaska's subsistence fight will finally head back to Congress next month, when the Senate Indian Affairs Committee is scheduled to hear a plea for action from Native leaders. An oversight hearing on subsistence has been set for Nov. 1 by the committee's chairman, Sen. Daniel Inouye, D- Hawaii. It will be the first congressional hearing on subsistence in nearly a decade. It's been even longer since a hearing on the subject was held in Washington, D.C. The hearing was requested by the Alaska Federation of Natives, according to spokesmen for Alaska's two senators, Frank Murkowski and Ted Stevens. Native leaders hope Congress can find a way around the state Legislature's resistance to a statewide subsistence priority for rural residents, AFN spokesman John Tetpon said this week. The hearing wi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Paint ball shooters target 5 ATTACKS: Latest spate doesn't appear racially motivated. By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 19, 2002) A group of people drove around the north part of Anchorage early Monday firing paint balls at pedestrians, police said. The incidents occurred between 12:30 and 1 a.m. Five victims reported being shot at in Fairview, downtown and Russian Jack. None were injured. They described seeing green paint balls shot out of a white Ford Explorer with tinted windows and occupied by at least three people. The victims included men and women of different races, and police said it doesn't appear bias motivated the assaults. These are the first paint ball shootings reported to Anchorage police since the January 2001 attacks in which Charles Wiseman, then…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Slow drivers may face higher fines ROADS: Going under speed limit, letting cars line up may cost $200. By Cathy Brown The Associated Press (Published: March 19, 2002) Juneau -- Driving too slowly could cost more than driving too fast under a bill that passed in the state Senate on Monday. The Senate voted 17-1 in favor of a bill to increase the fine for drivers who let five or more cars line up behind them from $30 to $200. The bill's sponsor, Sen. Dave Donley, R-Anchorage, said the law would apply only if the road in front of the driver is unobstructed, if he is traveling under the speed limit and if it's safe for him to pull over to let others pass. Donley said it's unfair and dangerous for a few drivers to "own" the road and set their own speed limit. "Unlike the Lower 48, Alaska …

title: Travel & Business info
summary: In Alaska Outdoors News Outdoor Hotline (Published: January 13, 2002) WORKSHOPS Exxon Valdez oil spill workshop set for Jan. 22-25 The 2002 Exxon Valdez Oil Spill annual workshop is scheduled for Jan. 22-25 at the Egan Center. A series of sessions is planned, including lingering oil spill injury, Gulf of Alaska ecosystem monitoring and the Southeast sustainable salmon fund. Registration is required. It is free. A public forum on marine mammals is set for 7 to 9 p.m. Jan. 22. It will feature presentations and discussions with biologist Craig Matkin on killer whales and contaminants, biologist Rosa Meehan on sea otter populations and biologist Tom Laughlin on Steller sea lion populations. For information or to register, contact Brenda Hall at 278-8012; fax at 276-7178; or e-mail at brenda_ha…

title: Travel & Business info
summary: In Alaska Outdoors News Snowmachine fee paying for upgrades to trails By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 13, 2002) At the Kroto Creek trail head on West Petersville road there are now portable latrines and an informational bulletin board for snowmachiners. In Clam Gulch and in the village of Chatanika, the local snowmachine clubs have new trail-grooming equipment. And near the small village of St. Michael a small emergency shelter is going to be built this year on a popular snowmachining trail. These are just a few of the more than 40 projects to improve snowmachining and promote snowmachine safety around the state that have been funded with more than $500,000 over the past three years. "It's a solid beginning," said Jim Stratton, director of the state Division of…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | In Brief (Published: March 19, 2002) GIRDWOOD Truck driver hurt, highway closed for 9 hours after tractor-trailer flips A tractor-trailer flipped over Monday afternoon on the Seward Highway just south of Girdwood, closing the road for more than nine hours. State troopers said Danny White, 36, of Seward was driving an Airland Transport double-trailer northbound at Mile 88. Troopers spokesman Greg Wilkinson said White came around a curve about 12:15 p.m. and his load of roofing materials shifted, causing the truck to flip over. Road conditions were reported as good and don't appear to have been a factor. White suffered injuries to his ribs and collarbone and was taken to an Anchorage hospital by the Girdwood Volunteer Fire Department. Airland sent a tow truck from Anchorage to remove t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: March 19, 2002) Deal to bring Japanese to Alaska As air carriers have dropped flights from Japan to the West Coast, often the first leg to Alaska, Hawaiian Vacations/Alaskan Vacations announced that it teamed up with a Japanese tour operator to offer flights to Alaska via Hawaii this summer. The agreement, with Japan Travel Bureau, will cover 47 departures from the beginning of June to the end of September, said David Karp, Hawaiian Vacations chief operating officer. Tourists will fly Northwest Airlines from Japan to Honolulu, then board a Hawaiian Vacations flight to Anchorage, Karp said. The Anchorage Convention and Visitors Bureau said it is pleased that Japanese travelers will have the Hawaii option, because Japan is an important tourism market …

title: Travel & Business info
summary: In Alaska News Alaska digest (Published: February 10, 2002) KETCHIKAN Craig man gets 75-year sentence for murdering man with maul A Craig man convicted of killing another man with a splitting maul was sentenced Friday to 75 years in prison with 25 suspended. Clinton Baker, 29, pleaded guilty in October to second-degree murder. He originally was charged with first-degree murder for the Sept. 11, 2000, killing of Michael Skrzynski, 46, who was living with Baker and Baker's mother on a float house in Craig. Baker fled after the murder and was found four days later on a beach on Fish Egg Island just off Craig. Prosecutor Dan Schally recommended a sentence of 50 to 60 years. Defense attorney Sue Crocker recommended a 25-year sentence. Craig is about 60 miles west of Ketchikan on the west side o…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Dead man's dog draws phone deluge ADOPTION: Buddy, the Lab whose owner was killed, draws 1,000 calls. By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 5, 2002) Owners of the Knight Island lodge that lost its caretaker in a tree-cutting accident last week said Monday they are close to finding a new home for the man's dog. Buddy became famous after he led searchers to the body of his owner, 45-year-old Bill Hitchcock. The dog had apparently waited near Hitchcock for 12 days after a chunk of tree he was cutting for firewood fell on him. Hitchcock had lived alone during the off-season on the Prince William Sound island, caring for the Sound Experience lodge, owned by Roger and Marilyn Stowell of Spokane, Wash. Buddy was his only company, and Hitchcock's death left the black Labrador …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Hawaiian Vacations only choice for nonstop TRAVEL: Anchorage residents have one choice for 5 1/2-hour trip. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 17, 2002) For many warmth- and light-deprived Anchorage residents, winter relief means two words: Hawaiian Vacations. With no one else offering direct flights from Anchorage to Hawaii, the company has a stronghold over the market. The fastest, and arguably the least expensive, way to get to the islands is by Hawaiian Vacations charter. The flight takes 5 1/2 hours and the lowest winter fare typically is $398, said Phil Okeson, chief financial officer. Alaska Airlines has never seriously considered offering nonstop service from Alaska to Hawaii, said spokesman Jack Evans. "When we look at growth, we look at continuing to …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | PURR RONDY 2002 (Published: February 25, 2002) Purr Rondy 2002 Cat Show "Best of the Best" category results listed by cat's name, breed, owner, city BEST KITTEN 1) Exoticrose Deniro, Bengal, Kathy Krysta/Shirley Piper, Riverside, Calif.; 2) Caravanserai Satine Tortitude, Turkish Van, Donna L. Ide, Anchorage; 3) Katabears Koala of Finerpoints, Himalayan, Kristine Webster, Murrieta, Calif. BEST CAT 1) Lunarcoons Moonlight of Denalicoons, Maine Coon, Richard/Valerie Johnston, Anchorage; 2) Purfur Ivarrived of Susitna, Havana, Harold/Elaine Law, Anchorage; 3) De's Cornex Lovey of Murch, Cornish Rex, Laura M. Saunders, Fairbanks BEST ALTER 1) Lightdancers Mauna Kea, Abyssinian, Janis M. Berry, Eagle River; 2) Caravanseri's Kashmir, Turkish Van, Donya Ide, Anchorage; 3) Exoticrose Warren B…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Power is money for Bush residents SUBSIDY: Higher electric bills chill rural households. By Danielle Wolffe Tundra Drums (Published: February 24, 2002) Bethel -- Tracy Rischer spent several nights last winter huddled up in blankets in a dark home because her electricity had been cut off. Rischer, 59, lives in Quinhagak, a coastal community 75 miles south of Bethel. She is among rural residents throughout Alaska who have difficulty managing the high cost of electricity every month. And this month her bills may be even higher because state power cost equalization funds have been reduced again. PCE was created in the early 1980s by legislators to bridge the gap between the costs of electricity between urban residents and many rural residents who pay two to three times more, said Meera K…

title: Fire kills Fairbanks 3-year-old
summary: Fairbanks -- A fire that killed a 3-year-old boy in a Fairbanks apartment complex Tuesday night began in the boy's bedroom and may have been caused by a butane lighter, the fire marshal's office said Wednesday. Passing motorists, neighbors and an Alaska State Trooper tried to rescue Cameron Lee Gray from the blazing second story of the apartment but were turned back by smoke. The boy's mother and two other children escaped the fire without injury. Trooper Sgt. Dave Drvenkar said the mother, Deven Gray, 23, was downstairs in the three-bedroom apartment with her 5-year-old son and 2-month-old baby when she heard Cameron screaming upstairs. "She ran upstairs and opened the bedroom door and heard what she described as a large vacuum and a gust of wind and then flames erupted," Drvenkar said. G…
